%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# B.6-2 Matrix Algebra

> **Section Objective:** Master three exam tasks — checking whether an operation is allowed, computing a matrix product, and solving for an unknown using an inverse.

---

Can a  2×3 matrix multiply a 3×2 matrix? **Yes** — inner dimensions match. Can it multiply a 2×2 matrix on the right? **No** — inner dimensions 3 and 2 do not match.

### WHAT TO MEMORIZE

- **Addition:** both matrices must have the same size.
- **Scalar multiplication:** always allowed.
- **Matrix multiplication  \(A_{m\times n} B_{n\times p}\):** inner dimensions must match; product is \(m\times p\).
- **Identity:** \(AI = IA = A\) when sizes fit.
- **Inverse \(A^{-1}\):** exists only for a square matrix with \(|A| \ne 0\).

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="exam_pattern_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 Matrix operation decision map: addition requires same size, scalar multiplication is always valid, and matrix multiplication requires matching inner dimensions with the product size determined by the outer dimensions.*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777214209340-1520.png)

$$A + B = (a_{ij} + b_{ij})_{m \times n}, \quad cA = Ac, \quad AB \text{ is defined only if } A_{m\times n} \text{ and } B_{n\times p}$$
*This is the operation checklist: addition requires both matrices to share the same size \(m\times n\), scalar multiplication scales every entry and is always valid, and matrix multiplication is defined only when the inner dimensions of \(A\) and \(B\) match.*

## 1. Matrix Multiplication: the row-by-column rule

The fastest exam workflow: **(1)** check dimensions, **(2)** predict the product size, **(3)** compute each entry.

### THE CORE PATTERN

Each entry \(c_{ij}\) of the product \(AB\) is the **row times column** dot product — row \(i\) of \(A\) dotted with column \(j\) of \(B\). This is **not** entrywise multiplication.

#### Example

If \(A\) is \(3\times 2\) and \(B\) is \(2\times 4\), the inner dimensions both equal 2, so \(AB\) is defined and the result is \(3\times 4\).

### EXAM TRAP

\(AB\) and \(BA\) are **not** generally equal. One may exist while the other is undefined. Never assume commutativity.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 Row-by-column multiplication: entry \(c_{ij}\) is the dot product of row \(i\) of \(A\) with column \(j\) of \(B\). The product size is determined by the outer dimensions.*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777214407435-4949.png)

$$c_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} a_{ik} b_{kj}$$
*Each entry \(c_{ij}\) of the product \(AB\) is computed as the dot product of row \(i\) of \(A\) with column \(j\) of \(B\), summing the products \(a_{ik}b_{kj}\) over all \(k\) from \(1\) to \(n\).*

## 2. Identity and Inverse: how matrix division really works

The **identity matrix** \(I\) acts like the number 1 for matrices: \(AI = IA = A\), but only when \(I\) has the correct size to make the multiplication conformable.

### MATRIX-VECTOR MULTIPLICATION

A column vector \(\mathbf{x}\) of length \(n\) is simply an \(n\times 1\) matrix. So \(A\mathbf{x}\) is valid whenever \(A\) has \(n\) columns. However, \(\mathbf{x}A\) is generally **not defined** in this context — do not flip the order.

### HIGH-YIELD INVERSE RULE

Given \(\mathbf{y} = A\mathbf{x}\), solve by premultiplying: \(\mathbf{x} = A^{-1}\mathbf{y}\).

> **Exam trap:** Square is necessary, nonzero determinant is also necessary. A square matrix with \(|A| = 0\) has no inverse and the system cannot be solved this way.

$$AI = IA = A, \quad y = Ax, \quad x = A^{-1}y, \quad A^{-1} \text{ exists only if } A \text{ is square and } |A| \ne 0$$
*The identity matrix leaves \(A\) unchanged on either side when sizes conform; a column vector \(\mathbf{x}\) multiplies as an \(n\times 1\) matrix so \(A\mathbf{x}\) is valid; and the inverse \(A^{-1}\) solves \(\mathbf{y} = A\mathbf{x}\) for \(\mathbf{x}\) only when \(A\) is square and nonsingular, i.e., \(|A| \ne 0\).*

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Addition needs same size; multiplication needs matching inner dimensions.
- Each entry of \(AB\) is a row-times-column dot product, not entrywise.
- \(A^{-1}\) exists only when \(A\) is square with \(|A| \ne 0\).

*In the next section we will apply these rules inside larger matrix-based problem solving.*
